coredata.dc:description: Integrating imaging and therapy is of great significance in the field of tumor treatment. Here, we report a polyvalent aptamer nanoplatform for breast cancer cell treatment based on rolling circle amplification (RCA). The platform consists of a long single-stranded DNA scaffold from RCA and short-stranded DNAs with various functions. One short strand is linked to near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs), another carries a MUC1 aptamer targeting cancer cells, and the third loads small interfering RNA (siRNA) for gene therapy. Doxorubicin (Dox) attaches to the platform via CG base pairs, integrating fluorescence targeting, photothermal therapy (PTT), gene therapy, and chemotherapy. Aptamer enhances breast cancer cell uptake of the platform. NIR-CDs serve as fluorescence imaging agents and are used for PTT. When deoxyribonucleases degrade the nanoplatform, Dox is released and then exerts its chemotherapeutic effect. SiRNA can form the RNA-induced silencing complex to reduce drug tolerance and enhance the efficacy of loaded drugs. The experimental results show that the platform can accumulate in cancer cells, exert a killing effect on them, and meanwhile reduce side effects on normal cells. In summary, this study expands the application of DNA materials in biomedicine by constructing a diagnostic and therapeutic platform that integrates NIR-CDs and RCA.

(A) TEM of NIR-CDs; (B) Gradually assemble 63 bases with each short strand (A1, A2, A3, S1, S2) to preliminarily verify the assembly; (C) Optimization of the ratio of RCA product to short-strand DNA for the self-assembly of IA; L1: Marker; L2: RCA product (L3: 1:5; L4: 1:10; L5: 1:30; L6: 1:50; L7: 1:70; L8: 1:100); (D) UV–Vis absorbance of NIR-CDs, A1, A1@NIR-CDs and A1@NIR-CDs (unconjugate); (E) FT-IR spectra of NIR-CDs and A1@NIR-CDs; (F) Zeta potential of NIR-CDs and A1@ NIR-CDs; (G) Temperature change of NIR-CDs, A1@NIR-CDs and IAN under irradiation of 808 nm near-infrared light (1.5 W/cm2, 10 min); (H) IR photographs under corresponding power densities (1: NIR-CDs, 2:A1@ NIR-CDs, 3: IAN); (I) Thermal curves of IAN during photothermal heating and cooling.

Molecular docking results of the interaction between Dox, NIR-CDs and DNA. (A) Mono-Assembly of the DNA double helix model; Molecular formulas of Dox (B) and NIR-CDs (C); (D) (I) Binding mode, (II) hydrogen bond length, (III) free Dox and (IV) conformation after interacting with DNA; (E) (I) Binding mode, (II) hydrogen bond length, (III) free NIR-CDs and (IV) conformation after interacting with DNA.

Investigation of the interaction between Dox, NIR-CDs and DNA. (A) RMSF of the between Dox, NIR-CDs and DNA; (B) RMSD of the between Dox, NIR-CDs and DNA; (C) Total potential energy of the between Dox, NIR-CDs and DNA; (D) Binding energy of the between Dox, NIR-CDs and DNA; (E) The influence of increasing the dosage of Dox on the relative viscosity of DNA; (F) The influence of increasing the dosage of NIR-CDs on the relative viscosity of DNA.

IAN specific target MCF-7 cells. (A) Fluorescence microscopy images (Left) of L929 and MCF-7 cells after incubation with NIR-CDs, MN and IAN for 30 min, and the corresponding Flow cytometric analysis (Right); (B) Conduct ImageJ analysis on the NIR-CDs signal in the Fluorescence microscopy images in (A). Scale bars: 10 μm.

(A) Cell viabilities of MCF-7 cells after photothermal ablation with different assemblies treatments (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2, 8 min); (B) Cell viabilities of L929 cells after photothermal ablation with different assemblies treatments (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2, 8 min); (C) Cell apoptosis stained by the Annexin V-FITC/PI assay (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2, 8 min) was analyzed by flow cytometry after various assemblies combined with NIR laser treatments. Data represent mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001.

Morphological and viability analysis on MCF-7 cells. (A) Live/dead cell staining images of L929 and MCF-7 cells following various treatments (808 nm, 1.5/cm2, 8 min); (B) MCF-7 cells scratch healing test following various treatments (808 nm, 1.5/cm2, 8 min). Scale bar: 100 μm.
Schematic illustration of preparation of polyvalent aptamer IAND and the mechanism of ablation cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Polyvalent aptamer DNA nanoassembly based near-infrared carbon dots and rolling circle amplification for synergistic therapy of breast cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Gan Ning and Fang Wang contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrating imaging and therapy is of great significance in the field of tumor treatment. Here, we report a polyvalent aptamer nanoplatform for breast cancer cell treatment based on rolling circle amplification (RCA). The platform consists of a long single-stranded DNA scaffold from RCA and short-stranded DNAs with various functions. One short strand is linked to near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs), another carries a MUC1 aptamer targeting cancer cells, and the third loads small interfering RNA (siRNA) for gene therapy. Doxorubicin (Dox) attaches to the platform via CG base pairs, integrating fluorescence targeting, photothermal therapy (PTT), gene therapy, and chemotherapy. Aptamer enhances breast cancer cell uptake of the platform. NIR-CDs serve as fluorescence imaging agents and are used for PTT. When deoxyribonucleases degrade the nanoplatform, Dox is released and then exerts its chemotherapeutic effect. SiRNA can form the RNA-induced silencing complex to reduce drug tolerance and enhance the efficacy of loaded drugs. The experimental results show that the platform can accumulate in cancer cells, exert a killing effect on them, and meanwhile reduce side effects on normal cells. In summary, this study expands the application of DNA materials in biomedicine by constructing a diagnostic and therapeutic platform that integrates NIR-CDs and RCA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous rise in cancer incidence, in the process of continuous exploration and innovation in modern medicine, scientific researchers are constantly pursuing integrated diagnosis and treatment plans for cancer that are highly efficient, are accurate, and have minimal side effects urgently [1,2]. Currently, theranostics has drawn significant attention from researchers. It integrates treatment and imaging functions on a nanoplatform and is employed for cancer imaging and drug delivery [3,4]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) represents an extremely promising emerging treatment modality. Through photothermal agents, light energy, particularly near-infrared light, is converted into heat energy to destroy diseased tissues [5,6]. In comparison with traditional treatment methods, PTT possesses the advantages of no drug resistance and non-invasiveness. Additionally, it can be easily combined with other methods to generate a synergistic effect, thereby enhancing the treatment outcome and significantly reducing damage to surrounding normal tissues [7–9]. As a new type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit excellent photothermal conversion efficiency, good biocompatibility, and stability [10–12]. Among them, near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) are particularly remarkable in PTT. They can convert light energy into heat energy to treat diseased tissues [13]. Their longer emission wavelengths are conducive to deep tissue detection and hold diagnostic potential. NIR-CDs play an outstanding tracer role and have positioning potential in integrating diagnosis and treatment [14,15]. Moreover, the photothermal treatment process is mild, which can effectively reduce the pain and complication risks associated with treatment [6].

DNA is a biological macromolecule and the carrier of biological genetic information [16]. It possesses excellent biocompatibility, programmable structure, and versatility. DNA can be designed to construct multifunctional DNA nanostructures, enabling the integration of functional sequences and efficient loading of multiple drugs [17–19]. For instance, the chemotherapy drug doxorubicin (Dox) can be embedded in the double helix structure of DNA [17], and the G-quadruplex generated under K+ can load protoporphyrin or heme [20]. Additionally, DNA nanostructures can be combined with various functional materials such as metal nanoparticles (e.g., AuNP) [21], carbon-based materials (e.g., CDs, carbon nanotubes) [22,23], and photosensitizers (e.g., photosensitizer, temoporfin) [24,25], expanding the applications of DNA nanotechnology in biomedicine and making it possible to construct cancer combination therapy nanoplatforms. In particular, since the surface of CDs is rich in various functional groups, such as amino groups, they can be connected to carboxyl-activated DNA through amidation reaction. [15,26]. Recently, rolling circle amplification (RCA) within isothermal enzyme amplification strategies has garnered increasing attention due to its advantages of high efficiency, simplicity, and universality [27]. By rationally designing the sequence of the circular template, various functional components such as DNA aptamers, drug-loading sites, and microenvironment response units can be directly integrated into the RCA product [20,28,29]. This enables polyvalent aptamer-targeted drug delivery, addressing the limitations of short-chain monovalent aptamers, including poor binding affinity, lack of stability, short duration of blood circulation half-life, and low drug loading capacity, and enhancing the precise targeting effect [30,31]. Therefore, the integration of RCA nanotechnology and NIR-CDs holds tremendous promise in biomedicine. As scientific research and technological innovation continue to advance, it is becoming more and more noticeable that this combination could potentially contribute to the field of biomedicine to some extent. Reasonably designed and precisely constructed multifunctional nanomaterials based on RCA are highly anticipated to jointly deliver reagents necessary for gene therapy, chemotherapy drugs, and other effective photothermal therapies for cancer treatment. These nanomaterials can be tailored to meet the specific needs of different types of cancer, providing targeted and personalized treatment options. As of now, despite the significant advancements in nanotechnology and biomedicine, the establishment of long-chain polyvalent aptamer nanomedicine delivery system for efficient targeted delivery of NIR-CDs to fulfill effective PTT has not been reported.

Herein, we presented a polyvalent aptamer IGF1R-Assembly@NIR-CDs + Dox (IAND) DNA nanoplatform that was based on the principle of RCA. This complex had the remarkable ability to co-deliver NIR-CDs, small interfering RNA (siRNA), and Dox, thereby achieving combined treatment of cancer (Scheme 1 ). Specifically, NIR-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and could be coupled to the A1 chain through amidation (Scheme 1a). Additionally, a long single-stranded rolling circle amplification (RCA) scaffold with repeating units was synthesized by a one-pot method. This scaffold self-assembled together with multiple complementary short chains with different functions to form a polyvalent aptamer. Meanwhile, Dox could be embedded in the double helix structure of DNA through CG base pairs (Scheme 1b). This multifunctional nano-drug delivery system contained MUC1 aptamer, which endowed it with the ability to specifically recognize the overexpressed MUC1 membrane protein on cancer cells. With the help of receptor-mediated endocytosis, the platform effectively entered the interior of cancer cells. The NIR-CDs in the multifunctional nano-drug delivery system had multiple functions. They were not only used as a targeted fluorescent group but also for PTT under 808 nm near-infrared laser irradiation. When various deoxyribonucleases in the cell metabolized IAND, Dox was released and entered the nucleus to inhibit DNA synthesis, thereby suppressing the proliferation of cancer cells. In addition, siRNA formed an RNA-induced silencing complex with related proteins, reducing the drug resistance of cells. It acted in concert with other treatment methods on cancer cells and ultimately induced cell apoptosis (Scheme 1c). In general, the proposed polyvalent aptamer IAND showcased several unique advantages. It possessed a precise targeted fluorescence imaging function, ensuring accurate visualization of cancer cells. Additionally, it had outstanding drug loading capacity, enabling the delivery of a sufficient amount of therapeutic agents. By effectively combining multiple therapeutic modalities, this nanoplatform brought significant enhanced effects for cancer treatment. It held great potential in the field of biomedical applications and may have paved the way for more advanced cancer therapies in the future.

N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide (APAA), 4,5-difuorobenzene-1,2-diamine (DFPD), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), and 1-methyl imidazole were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai China). All DNA sequences listed in Table S1 were purchased from GenScript (Jiangsu China). RPMI-1640 medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and a combination of penicillin and streptomycin were sourced from Thermo Fisher Scientific (MD, USA). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Millipore Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ resistivity) was used throughout the experiments.

A U-3900H (HITACHI) was used to obtain the UV–Vis spectra. A Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific) was used to obtain FT-IR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) spectra. The fluorescence spectra were acquired using an F-7100 (HITACHI). A JEM-1400Plus TEM (JEOL) was used to obtain TEM images and the Zeta potential was measured with Zeta Pals (Brookhaven). The BioTek synergy H1 microplate reader (USA) was utilized to assess cell viability. Cell imaging was carried out by a IX73 inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus). Flow cytometry analysis was conducted using a BD AccuriC6 Plus instrument.

NIR-CDs were synthesized in accordance with previous work [32]. Specifically, APAA (0.15 g) and DFPD (0.047 g) were mixed in 20 mL ultrapure water and subjected to ultrasonic dissolution. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h. Then, the obtained NIR-CDs were purified by centrifugation with a high-speed centrifuge for 10 min. After that, the obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to avoid the presence of large particles. The filtrate was then dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da for one day. After dialysis was completed, the liquid inside the dialysis bag was taken and solid powder was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A1 sequence coupled with NIR-CDs (A1@ NIR-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The coupling of A1 sequence was carried out according to the previous work [33]. Specifically, 10 μL 5′-phosphorylated single-stranded DNA at different concentrations (0.5, 1.625, 3.75, 7.5, 15 μM) was mixed with 10 μL EDC (10 mM) and 10 μL 1-methylimidazole (20 mM) aqueous solution at pH 6, and then stirred continuously at 37 °C for 90 min. Subsequently, in the presence of 10 μL HEPES buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 8), NIR-CDs (10 μL, 5 mg/mL) were added to the activated 5′-phosphorylimidazole single-stranded DNA and the temperature was maintained at 55 °C and kept at this level for 18 h. Finally, dialysis was employed to purify the product and eliminate excessive EDC and 1-methylimidazole. The concentration of the purified product was determined using a Nano 100 micro-spectrophotometer.

The synthesis of circular template was carried out according to our previous work [34]. Specifically, phosphorylated the linear template strand in a 50 μL reaction mixture (including 5 μL of 10 × T4 polynucleotide kinase buffer, 1 μL T4 polynucleotide kinase, 10 μL 1 mM ATP, 5 μL 100 μM linear template and 29 μL H2O). Incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to transfer the phosphate group. Heated at 90 °C for 5 min to inactivate the T4 polynucleotide kinase. Then added 5 μL 100 μM primer and 33 μL H2O. Heated at 95 °C for 10 min and then cooled it naturally to 25 °C for 3 h hybridization. After hybridization, added 10 μL 10 × T4 DNA ligase buffer and 2 μL T4 DNA ligase. Incubated at 16 °C overnight and then heated at 65 °C for 10 min to inactivate it. Then added exonuclease I and III and the corresponding buffer. Incubated at 37 °C for 3 h and then heated at 80 °C for 15 min to inactivate it. Finally, purified the circular template by ethanol precipitation and characterized it by 3 % agarose gel electrophoresis.

63 base sequence was the repeating unit of RCA product. 63 base sequence was mixed with different complementary short DNA strands (A1, A2, A3, S1 and S2 strands) in 1 × TE-Mg2+ buffer. Then the temperature was lowered from 95 °C (held for 5 min) to 25 °C by using a PCR instrument. Finally, the product was verified by 3 % agarose gel electrophoresis.

MN: Under the condition of pH 6, Mono-assembly was mixed with 10 mM EDC and 20 mM 1-methylimidazole aqueous solution for activation. Then it was reacted for 90 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, in HEPES buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 8), NIR-CDs were added to the activated IA at 55 °C for 18 h. Finally, the product was purified to obtain MN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of RCA product and IGF1R-assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The RCA product was performed according to our previous work [35]. Centrifuged and mixed evenly the 20 μL reaction mixture (2 μL circular template, 2 μL 10 × Phi29 DNA polymerase buffer, 2 μL DNA primer, 2 μL dNTPs, 0.5 μL Phi29 DNA polymerase and 11.5 μL H₂O). Then incubated it in polymerase chain reaction (PCR) machine for 25 min at 30 °C. After the incubation, inactivated the Phi29 DNA polymerase at 65 °C. When it cooled down to 25 °C, the RCA product could be obtained.

IGF1R-assembly (IA): The long single strand (ssDNA) generated by RCA was dispersed in 1 × TE-Mg2+ buffer together with A1, A2, A3, S1 and S2 (the ratio of ssDNA to each complementary short strand was 1:50). Then the temperature was cooled from 95 °C (kept for 5 min) to 25 °C by PCR instrument to obtain IA.

Under pH 6, IA was mixed with 10 mM EDC and 20 mM 1-methylimidazole aqueous solution for activation. Then it was reacted for 90 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, in HEPES buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 8), NIR-CDs (5 mg/mL) were added to the activated IA at 55 °C for 18 h. Finally, the product was purified to obtain IAN.
As for IAND, IAN with 1.5 nM and 300 nM Dox were incubated at 37 °C (30 min) to obtain IAND.

Molecular docking result could provide crucial information on drug DNA binding at the molecular level. With the help of Autodock 4.2.3 software, an in - depth analysis was carried out on the possible binding conformations and intermolecular forces formed by NIR-CDs and Dox with DNA respectively. Specifically, first, treatments such as dehydration and hydrogenation were performed on the ligands and receptors to ensure the accuracy and stability of the system. Subsequently, the Lamarckian genetic algorithm was used to carry out docking operations in a three - dimensional spatial grid box with a spacing of 0.375 Å and a size of 126 × 126 × 126. This algorithm could obtain reliable docking results. PyMol software was used to conduct a visual analysis of the conformation corresponding to the lowest binding energy. Through this process, the interaction patterns between NIR-CDs and DNA, and between Dox and DNA could be clearly observed.

MD simulation was helpful for studying drug-ligand complexes. This simulation inferred the kinetic properties of drug molecules from a computational perspective by mimicking the physiological conditions of drugs. The system input files for the MD simulation were generated by Charmm-gui. The MD simulation of drug-ligand complexes was carried out using GROMACS 2020.6 software under the action of the selected CHARMM36m force field. Referring to previous literature [36], 100 ns of MD simulations were respectively conducted on Dox and DNA, as well as NIR-CDs and DNA.

DSC experiments were conducted using a Q20 differential scanning calorimeter from TA Instruments (USA). In the experiments, the molar concentration of DNA was 0.075 μM, Dox was 3 μM, and NIR-CDs was 100 μg/mL. First, scan the sample and reference cells with PBS buffer for the baseline. Then, stir and degas the sample solution thoroughly before adding it to the sample cell. Set the temperature range from 20 to 110 °C and the scanning rate at 1 °C·min−1.

Viscosity was measured using a Ubbelohde viscometer placed in a constant - temperature water bath at 25 °C. A stopwatch measured the solution's capillary flow time, with each sample measured thrice. The DNA concentration was fixed at 5 × 10−5 mol/L. Flow times of the DNA solution and DNA solutions with varying drug concentrations were measured to get C Drug/C DNA. Data were presented as a plot of (η/η0)1/3 against C Drug/C DNA, where η0 is the viscosity of the DNA - only solution and η is that of the DNA solution with Dox or NIR-CDs.

Gel electrophoresis was employed for the analysis of the assembly and the optimization of the concentration ratio. 0.6 % agarose gel was prepared with 1 × TAE buffer. Then, 2 μL loading buffer and 8 μL the assemblies were added. After that, electrophoresis was carried out at 300 V for 60 min. Finally, gel imaging was performed.

Under the condition of 808 nm near-infrared laser, the photothermal effect of the water suspension of materials was explored. At the same time, with the help of an infrared thermal imager, under the condition of different laser intensities, it was continuously observed for 10 min every 30 s. To deeply study the photothermal stability, the NIR-CDs and IAN suspensions were placed in the NIR laser, and then they were allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. This process was repeated for 5 cycles.

L929 cells (mouse fibroblast cells) and MCF-7 cells (human breast cancer cells) were obtained from Beijing Beinacron Institute of Biotechnology in Beijing, China. The aforementioned cell lines were grown in RPMI-1640 medium that had been supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin. Then, the L929 and MCF-7 cells were kept in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO₂ at a constant temperature of 37 °C.

The biocompatibility of nanomaterials on L929 and MCF-7 cells was assessed by CCK-8 method. First, L929 and MCF-7 cells were inoculated into 96 well plates (5 × 103/well) and exposed to the materials at different concentrations for different times. Then, 808 nm laser irradiation and incubation were carried out as appropriate for a certain period of time. After that, CCK8 reagent was added and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured to evaluate the vitality of the cells. The results obtained from three repeated experiments were averaged. This average value represented the percentage of cell survival rate relative to the control group.

L929 and MCF-7 cells were seeded on glass-bottomed dishes and cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 overnight. They were then treated with various material concentrations for different durations. After washing with PBS, cells were fixed with 10 % paraformaldehyde for 10 min, washed again, and stained with DAPI for 15 min. Following further PBS washes, images were taken with an inverted fluorescence microscope. Fluorescence data were analyzed using ImageJ software.

The cellular uptake efficiency was examined quantitatively through flow cytometry. L929 and MCF-7 cells were grown in 96 well plates overnight. The cells were then treated with various material. Afterward, the cells were thoroughly washed with PBS buffer to remove any unbound material and separated using trypsin-EDTA. The separated cells were resuspended in PBS buffer and subjected to flow cytometry analysis using a BD AccuriC6 Plus instrument.

The apoptosis of MCF-7 cells after photothermal treatment was observed by flow cytometry. MCF-7 cells (5 × 103/well) were cultured on 96 well plates for 24 h. Different materials were added to each well and co-incubated with the cells for 6 h. Then, NIR irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) was performed for 8 min. The cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI Cell Apoptosis Detection Kit (Yeasen Biotech Co., Ltd.). The cells were analyzed by a BD AccuriC6 Plus instrument.

To observe the effect of anti-tumor, live/dead cell staining was performed first under the irradiation of near-infrared light. Specifically, L929 and MCF-7 cells were cultured in a 96 well plate and incubated with different materials for 6 h so that the cells could take up. Then, the cells were carefully washed with PBS buffer. After that, they were irradiated with 808 nm near-infrared laser with a power of 1.5 W/cm2 for 8 min, and then incubated for 24 h. Finally, the cells were stained with calcein AM and PI, and imaged by a fluorescence microscope.

To evaluate the migration ability of cells, L929 and MCF-7 cells were seeded in a 96 well plate at a density of 5 × 103 per well and incubated overnight. Then, the monolayer cells were scraped with a 10 μL pipette tip. To remove the suspended cells, they were washed 3 times with PBS. Finally, L929 and After MCF-7 cells were incubated in culture media containing different drugs for 6 h, the culture media were removed, and then irradiated 8 min with 808 nm laser. After that, the inverted fluorescence microscope was used for observation and image acquisition at different times.
Statistical analysis was performed with a two-tailed unpaired t-test. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001.

Super-long single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) was synthesized via RCA for efficient loading of siRNA and Dox. Three functional sequences were ingeniously designed in ssDNA: complementary sequences with modified phosphate groups, the recognition sequence of MUC1, and the siRNA primer sequence. Specifically, the DNA sequence with modified phosphate groups bound to NIR-CDs through phosphoramidation. NIR-CDs acted as a photothermal agent and a fluorescent targeting agent. The recognition sequence of MUC1 bound to the overexpressed MUC1 protein in cancer cells, enhancing the active targeting ability. The drug Dox bound to the CG base pair in the DNA double strand by intercalation. The siRNA primer sequence efficiently loaded the siRNA sequence. The loaded interfering sequence was the siRNA of insulin-like growth factor receptor (IGF-1R), which increased the sensitivity of cancer cells to drugs and promoted cancer cell apoptosis [37]. After the polyvalent aptamer IAND was internalized by cells, the fluorescence of NIR-CDs tracked the targeting situation of the assembly. Once inside the cell, various deoxyribonucleases in the cell decomposed the IAND complex. The released NIR-CDs generated photothermal heat under 808 nm laser irradiation to kill cancer cells. Dox performed chemotherapy accurately, and siRNA achieved epidermal growth factor receptor silencing induction, making tumor cells more sensitive to chemotherapy drugs and realizing synergistic treatment.

NIR-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. NIR-CDs were well dispersed in solution and presented quasi-spherical (Fig. 1A). Fig. S1A showed that the optimal emission wavelength of NIR-CDs is 675 nm under excitation at 530 nm. The fluorescence emission peak was almost uncorrelated with the change of excitation wavelength, and the maximum emission wavelength and full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission peak showed consistency, indicating the excitation independence and uniform morphology of NIR-CDs [32,38]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S1B, NIR-CDs had extensive light absorption in the visible and infrared ranges. The absorption peaks at approximately 251 nm and 520 nm were derived from the π → π* transition of the CC bond and the n → π* transition of the CO and/or C–O/C–N bonds [32].

In view of the fact that NIR-CDs had light absorption characteristics within a wide ultraviolet absorption range, they therefore had adjustable photothermal effects [39]. As shown in Fig. S2A, in order to enhance the ability to penetrate tissues, an 808 nm laser was selected. The temperature changes of NIR-CDs at different concentrations under laser irradiation at 1.5 W/cm2 were explored. The temperature increased from 15.58 °C (25 μg/mL) to 28.86 °C (200 μg/mL). In contrast, the temperature of pure water only rose by 7.58 °C (Fig. S2B). The infrared imaging (IR) results further confirmed the temperature changes of different materials (Fig. S2C). At the same time, the temperature changes of NIR-CDs (100 μg/mL) under different powers were also explored (Fig. S2D). The results showed that the temperature rose rapidly with the increase of laser power. At 1.5 W/cm2, with a concentration of 100 μg/mL and an irradiation time of 8 min, the temperature could reach 45 °C. IR also confirmed this point (Fig. S2F). For tumor PTT, a temperature of 43–45 °C can effectively kill tumor cells and reduce damage to normal tissues. Therefore, 1.5 W/ cm2 and 100 μg/mL were selected for the next step of the experiment. The photothermal stability of NIR-CDs was explored through 5 laser on/off cycles (Fig. S2E). The temperature change of the solution (100 μg/mL) over time under continuous irradiation of an 808 nm laser (1.5 W/cm2) was recorded. It was found that the maximum temperature of NIR-CDs did not change significantly during these cycles. According to the obtained data (Fig. S2G and H), the photothermal conversion efficiency of NIR-CDs could be calculated to be about 26.2 %.

First, a 63 base repeat unit was used to verify whether the assembly could be assembled step by step. As depicted in Fig. 1B, as each short chain is gradually added, the brightness of the band and the molecular weight gradient progressively increase, which indicated the successful establishment of the assembly. Next, under the action of primers and T4 DNA ligase, the linear template was converted into a circular template. Compared with the linear template, the molecular weight of the circular template was increased, which meant that it was successfully prepared (Fig. S3A) [20]. ssDNA was synthesized by RCA reaction. As shown in Fig. S3B, the RCA product had a large molecular weight, indicating the successful synthesis of ultra-long ssDNA. The optimal molar concentration ratio of ssDNA to 5 short DNA chains (sDNA) was optimized. The results demonstrated that when the concentration ratio of ssDNA to sDNA was 1:50, the comprehensive cost and efficiency were the best (Fig. 1C). Therefore, this ratio was selected as the subsequent assembly ratio.

Subsequently, the coupling of the A1 short chain modified with phosphate groups to NIR-CDs was investigated, and the A1 short chain coupled to NIR-CDs was analyzed by UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy, FT-IR spectra, Zeta potential, agarose gel electrophoresis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1D showed that compared with single NIR-CDs and A1 short chains, the UV–Vis absorbance peak of A1@NIR-CDs at 264 nm was red-shifted to 277 nm [33]. Furthermore, in the infrared spectrum (Fig. 1E), after the A1 short chain was grafted to NIR-CDs, a double peak at 1203 cm−1 and 1538 cm−1 was found in the spectrum of A1@NIR-CDs, indicating the existence of the phosphoryl group (P=O) and amino group (NH) of the phosphoramide bond (—P(=O) (—NH—)) formed in the AS1411 aptamer binding process. In addition, the characteristic peak at 1012 cm−1 belonged to the OP stretching vibration of the nucleic acid phosphate group [33,40]. Since NIR-CDs were positively charged (+23.6 mV), while DNA was negatively charged, the charge of the two after coupling was still positive, which to a certain extent provided evidence for A1@NIR-CDs (Fig. 1F). Moreover, the hydrodynamic diameter of A1@NIR-CDs was slightly larger than that of NIR-CDs (Fig. S4). This might have been due to the conjugation of A1 and NIR-CDs. Then, by fixing the concentration of NIR-CDs (100 μg/mL) and coupling with different concentrations of A1 short chains, the results showed that NIR-CDs could carry A1 short chains to migrate in the opposite direction (Fig. S5). In conclusion, it was proved that A1 could be coupled with NIR-CDs.

After that, whether IAN could be successfully assembled was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, compared with the assembly of the A1 chain modified with NIR-CDs, IA was blocked in the gel pore. However, IAN formed a positively charged complex due to coupling with NIR-CDs and migrated in the opposite direction under the influence of electric field force. Moreover, its migration rate was slightly slower than that of single NIR-CDs, which proved that IAN could be formed [35]. Moreover, compared with the good dispersibility of the bare NIR-CDs, IAN presented a regular linear arrangement, which further confirmed the formation of IAN (Fig. S7). Next, the fluorescence properties and photothermal properties of IAN were explored. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence of IAN did not change much compared with single NIR-CDs, which proved that it had potential targeted fluorescence ability. In addition, under laser irradiation at 1.5 W/cm2, the temperature changes of IAN, single NIR-CDs and A1@NIR-CDs were recorded (Fig. 1G). The results showed that IAN had a certain photothermal enhancement effect (approximately 4 °C), the IR results further confirmed the temperature changes of different materials (Fig. 1H, which might be because the assembly caused NIR-CDs to aggregate, increasing the particle size and correspondingly enhancing the photothermal effect [41]. The photothermal stability of IAN was explored through 5 laser on/off cycles (Fig. 1I), and the temperature change of the solution with time under continuous irradiation of 808 nm laser (1.5 W/cm2) was recorded. It was found that the maximum temperature of IAN did not change significantly during these cycles. According to the obtained data (Fig. S9), the photothermal conversion efficiency of IAN could be calculated to be about 32.2 %. In conclusion, the prepared IAN nanoplatform had good photothermal properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction patterns of Dox and NIR-CDs with RCA product
ce:section.ce:para: In the field of drug delivery, the docking of nanomaterials and drug molecules was expected to improve the bioavailability of drugs, reduce toxic and side effects, and achieve targeted therapy. Therefore, molecular docking was used to explore the interaction forces and binding sites between Dox and NIR-CDs and DNA. First, the DNA crystal structure of the mono-assembled DNA double helix was obtained by base complementarity from the Protein Data Bank with the repeating sequence of RCA products (63 base sequence) (Fig. 2A). The model diagram of NIR-CDs was constructed according to the previous literature (Fig. 2B) [42].

The findings of molecular docking Dox and NIR-CDs with DNA were presented as Fig. 2D and E, respectively. The docking binding energy between Dox and DNA was −8.2 kcal/mol, and the intermolecular energies of van der Waals force, hydrogen bond, desolvation energy and electrostatic energy were − 11.48 kcal/mol. Fig. 2D showed that the tetracyclic structure of Dox was inserted into the base pair. The -NH2 at the C33 position of Dox formed a hydrogen bond with the O atom of the 27th base T, and the bond length was 1.8 Å; the O atom at the C2 position formed a hydrogen bond with the 21st H of the 39th base G, and the bond length was 1.9 Å; the O at the C8 position formed a hydrogen bond with the 3rd H atom of the 40th base A, and the bond length was 1.8 Å. III and IV in Fig. 2D showed the conformations of free Dox and Dox that has interacted with DNA were presented respectively. The amino sugar group was distorted, which had an impact on its conformation [43]. These results preliminarily confirmed that the interaction mode between Dox and DNA is intercalation [44]. The intermolecular energy between NIR-CDs and DNA was −2.73 kcal/mol. The O atom at the C23 position of NIR-CDs formed a hydrogen bond with the second hydrogen atom and the twenty-first hydrogen atom at the forty-third position, and the bond length was 2.1 Å. At the same time, the side chain was twisted after docking, causing a change in the conformation of NIR-CDs. The docking results indicated that electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bond played an important role in the binding of NIR-CDs and DNA. The molecular docking results showed that both NIR-CDs and Dox could bind to DNA and form hydrogen bonds with the bases in DNA. Because the binding energy between DNA and Dox was low, the binding between DNA and Dox was more stable than that of NIR-CDs.

In addition, MD simulations and viscosity experiments were carried out to further verify the binding modes of Dox and NIR-CDs with DNA, respectively. In the MD simulations, the root - mean - square fluctuation (RMSF) plot was a powerful tool for analyzing the dynamic fluctuations of atoms or residues. Residues in regions of high fluctuation were more likely to undergo conformational changes. Such changes could be due to external stimuli or participation in intermolecular interactions [45]. As shown in Fig. 3A, in the RMSF plot of the combined action of Dox, NIR-CDs, and DNA, the regions of high residue fluctuations were highly consistent with the results presented by molecular docking. The root - mean - square deviation (RMSD) plot was one of the important bases for determining whether the simulation system had reached an equilibrium state. When the RMSD value fluctuated slightly above and below a certain average value over a period of time, it indicated that the simulation system had approached equilibrium [46]. As could be seen from Fig. 3B, the RMSD values of both Dox and NIR-CDs reached an equilibrium state, and the subsequent fluctuation amplitude was relatively small. This result showed that the data generated from this simulation were highly reliable and could be used for further in - depth analysis. In addition, the total potential energy plots of Dox and NIR-CDs (Fig. 3C) also exhibited the characteristic of fluctuating slightly around a relatively stable average value (−108 × 103 kJ/mol), which fully demonstrated that the entire system was in a relatively stable state. As could be observed from Fig. 3D, the binding energy obtained from the MD simulation was basically consistent with that obtained from molecular docking. In addition, the intercalation of Dox and NIR-CDs with DNA was further confirmed by measuring DNA's melting temperature (Tm) via DSC [47]. Fig. S10 showed DNA's thermal denaturation curves before and after loading Dox and NIR-CDs. DNA had a single endothermic peak (80.2 °C) during thermal denaturation. When Dox and NIR-CDs interacted with DNA, the endothermic peak temperatures rose to 105.4 °C and 96.1 °C respectively. This was due to ligand molecules intercalating into the DNA double - helix, enhancing its thermal stability and raising the Tm value. The result was consistent with the above analysis.

During the study of the interaction between small molecules and DNA, viscosity was a parameter that could effectively distinguish the binding modes of the two. When small molecules interacted with DNA in an intercalative manner, because the intercalative binding required a relatively large space from adjacent base pairs, the double - helix structure of DNA was stretched during this process, which in turn led to a significant increase in the viscosity of the DNA solution. In contrast, when small molecules interacted with DNA through electrostatic interactions or groove - binding modes, the impact on the DNA structure was relatively limited, so the change in the viscosity of DNA was not significant [44]. As shown in Fig. 3E, as the concentration of Dox increased, the relative viscosity of DNA increased accordingly. This phenomenon fully indicated that the binding mode of Dox with DNA was intercalative binding. In sharp contrast, when the concentration of NIR-CDs increased, the relative viscosity of DNA did not change significantly (Fig. 3F). Thus, it was speculated that the binding mode of NIR-CDs with DNA was most likely electrostatic interaction or groove - binding modes. This conclusion was completely consistent with the results of molecular docking. Compared with existing drugs (Table S2), Dox and NIR-CDs could bind to DNA through different binding modes respectively, thereby exerting their effects, and achieved the expected effects more effectively. In addition, the drugs reported in other literature usually adopted a single binding mode [44,48], and the therapeutic effects were also relatively single. In sharp contrast, Dox and NIR-CDs showed complementarity when acting on cancer cells. Inside the cancer cells, the two could interfere with the physiological activities of cancer cells from different levels and achieve synergistic treatment.

Dox had a planar tetracyclic structure and could be inserted into the base pairs of DNA double helix structure through π-π stacking interaction [49–51], so it could load drugs with high efficiency. Therefore, the ability of the assembly to load Dox was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S11A and B, based on the standard curve of Dox and the calculation results, the loading capacity of IAN for Dox could reach nearly 90 %. In addition, DNase I was widely present in animal cells. Therefore, the behavior of DNase I degrading IAN to release drugs was explored. The results showed that the drug loaded by the assembly could be released under the action of DNase I (Fig. S11C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on in vitro biosafety and cellular intake
ce:section.ce:para: Before applying IAN to the biomedical field, it was necessary to confirm the toxicity of NIR-CDs and RCA products. So, cytotoxicity tests were conducted on L929 and MCF-7 cells by CCK-8. After co-culturing NIR-CDs with the cells for 24 h and 48 h, the cell viability was still >85 % at a concentration of 300 μg/mL, indicating low cytotoxicity (Fig. S12). When RCA products at different concentrations were co-cultured with L929 and MCF-7 cells, the cells showed good survival conditions, demonstrating good biocompatibility (Fig. S13). NIR-CDs had excellent optical properties and biocompatibility and could be used in applications such as cell imaging. As could be seen from Fig. S14, after incubating with the cells for 8 h, it emitted strong red fluorescence, effectively resisting interference from autofluorescence and highlighting its unique advantages and reliability in cell imaging. It was particularly noteworthy that NIR-CDs could stay steadily in the cell for 24 h without excretion after entering the cell (Fig. S15). This indicated that it had excellent stability in the intracellular environment. It was conducive to fluorescence imaging and serving as a photothermal agent.

Then, the targeting ability of IAN to MCF-7 cells was evaluated by an inverted fluorescence microscope. After incubating MCF-7 cells for 30 min, its internalization effect was stronger than that of NIR-CDs and MN, with more obvious red fluorescence. However, in each control group of L929 cells undergoing incubation, there was hardly any red fluorescence (Fig. 4A). Moreover, the fluorescence quantification analysis of ImageJ (Fig. 4B) and the results of flow cytometry (Fig. 4A right) all supported this finding, indicating that IAN had the potential for rapidly targeting cancer cells. The rapid targeting of IAN was likely due to the fact that it was a polyvalent aptamer, capable of carrying more MUC1 aptamers and having higher affinity for MUC1 protein overexpressed on the cell surface [30]. In addition, due to its small size, strong permeability and positive charge, NIR-CDs facilitated the interaction with the cell membrane and enhanced its internalization and targeting capabilities [52].

To achieve the best effect of combined anti-tumor, the concentration of Dox was explored. As shown in the Fig. S16, when the concentration of Dox was 300 nM and incubated for 24 h, the survival rate of MCF-7 cells was 70 %. Considering the influence of the factor that the combined treatment and RCA products could load Dox at a ratio of nearly 1:200, the concentration of RCA was selected as 1.5 nM. Then, the photothermal combined anti-tumor effect of various NIR-CDs components was evaluated by CCK8. As shown in Fig. 5A, in the absence of laser irradiation, various assemblies including NIR-CDs, MN, Assembly@NIR-CDs (AN), Assembly@NIR-CDs + Dox (AND) and IAND did not cause serious damage to MCF-7 cells. However, in contrast, the cell viability of each assembly exposed to 808 nm laser decreased to a certain extent, which was due to their photothermal effect. Fig. 5B showed that these same assemblies had no significant killing effect on normal cells because the cancer cells had strong vitality and could absorb more assemblies. In addition, due to the targeting effect of polyvalent aptamer, more assemblies accumulated in MCF-7 cells. In addition, in the IAND treatment group, the PTT result was enhanced, and the MCF-7 cells viability of this group dropped to about 25 %. This indicated that gene therapy enhanced the sensitivity of cancer cells to chemotherapeutic drugs, improved the chemotherapeutic effect, and thus greatly improved the PTT performance. Overall, under the irradiation of an 808 nm laser, the survival rate of normal cells treated with NIR-CDs reached 83 %, indicating that NIR-CDs caused relatively little damage to normal cells. In addition, with the action of polyvalent aptamer, their targeting ability to cancer cells was significantly enhanced. This allowed for the effective killing of cancer cells while reducing the killing effect on normal cells to a certain extent.

Next, in order to fully understand the anti-tumor mechanism of IAND, Annexin V-FITC/PI fluorescence staining of cell apoptosis induced by various assemblies was performed and analyzed by flow cytometry system. As shown in Fig. 5C, in the group with PBS (laser) and the group treated with NIR-CDs (free laser) alone, >96 % of the cells were distributed in the lower left quadrant, indicating that the cells were in a good living state after the above treatment. However, after laser irradiation, the apoptosis rates of cells treated with incubated NIR-CDs, MN, AN, AND and IAND reached 33.2 %, 43.8 %, 57 %, 66.7 % and 72.2 % respectively, because the photothermal ablation effect of NIR-CDs on tumor cells played a dominant role. The apoptosis rates of NIR-CDs, MN and AN groups increased gradually because the modification of aptamers increased the cell uptake and thereby improving the cell apoptosis rate. The cell apoptosis rate of IAND was higher than that of AND, which further confirmed that gene therapy enhanced the sensitivity of cancer cells to chemotherapeutic drugs, improved the chemotherapeutic effect, and thus improved the PTT performance. In general, the data from flow cytometry showed that the cells treated with various assemblies showed unrepairable damage after photothermal treatment and had an effective ablation effect on tumor cells.

Moreover, we used a co-staining method of calcein AM (green) and PI (red) to distinguish live cells from dead cells (Fig. 6A). Under the condition of 808 nm laser irradiation, both L929 cells and MCF-7 cells in the Control group had good survival rates. However, in the groups of NIR-CDs, MN, AN, AND and IAND, the red fluorescence of MCF-7 cells showed a gradually increasing trend, and at the same time, their cell density was continuously decreasing. Under the action of the same assembly, the green fluorescence and cell density of L929 cells did not show obvious changes. This clearly indicated that IAND containing polyvalent aptamer could extremely effectively target MCF-7 cells and accumulate in cells to exert a significant anti-cancer effect. In addition, the combined anti-cancer effect of IAND was further confirmed by the scratch healing test. As shown in Fig. 6B, the scratch widths of the other MCF-7 cells control groups gradually narrowed, while the scratch width of the IAND group hardly changed. This indicated that under the combined action of PTT, gene therapy and chemotherapy, the migration of MCF-7 cells was significantly inhibited. After PTT destroyed diseased tissue, at the cellular level, the cell membrane and organelles were damaged and cells died. At the tissue level, the structure disintegrated. Due to the body's immune regulatory mechanism, an uncontrolled immune response usually did not occur [53,54].

In this article, a polyvalent aptamer DNA nanoplatform (IAND) was successfully constructed. This assembly was designed to load NIR-CDs, Dox, and siRNA onto DNA, aiming to achieve highly efficient combined therapy for cancer cells. Through various methods such as molecular docking, MD simulation, gel electrophoresis, and viscosity experiments, it was confirmed that Dox and NIR-CDs could bind to DNA in different binding modes. As detected by fluorescence imaging and flow cytometry, the constructed assembly was found to be able to rapidly target and recognize cancer cells. In addition, IAND can release Dox in a responsive manner under the action of intracellular deoxyribonuclease, and jointly exert a combined therapeutic effect with NIR-CDs and siRNA. The CCK8 assay showed IAND achieved about 75 % cancer cell killing rate. The flow cytometry apoptosis assay demonstrated that IAND could induce apoptosis in 72.2 % of cancer cells. In addition, both the live/dead cell assay and the wound healing assay also confirmed the anti-cancer effect of IAND. This study correlated the drug binding mechanism with drug release and therapeutic efficacy, providing a theoretical basis for the combined therapy of NIR-CDs based nano DNA assemblies.

Although this study has demonstrated that IAND has considerable potential in targeting and killing cancer cells at the cellular level, there are still some limitations. For example, the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Given the limited experimental scope, further in-depth research is needed to clarify these mechanisms. Moreover, exploring the interaction between NIR-CDs and DNA is crucial for understanding the pharmacological properties of NIR-CDs, which may help develop NIR-CDs into a new anti-tumor drug in the future. Future work mainly focuses on: (1) Deeply studying and analyzing the ablation mechanism of NIR-CDs and DNA-based nanomaterials on cancer cells, their side effects on normal cells, and in vivo metabolic processes. (2) Developing new “integrated” photothermal reagents to endow nanomaterials with photothermal, photodynamic, and chemotherapy synergistic effects and multi-modal imaging capabilities for optimal diagnosis and treatment outcomes. Looking ahead, exploring clinical applications, optimizing materials for multifunctional integration, and expanding application fields are important development directions. These efforts may bring new breakthroughs to the diagnosis and treatment of cancer and other diseases.
